_meta.what_firm_does: Faire is an online wholesale marketplace connecting independent retailers (local boutiques, shops) with brands and makers, so small retailers can compete with big-box stores. Mission: level the playing field for independent retail.
_meta.jd_wants: Build trustworthy People data — definitions, governance, dashboards, and self-service — and partner with People + IT to shape an AI roadmap for People tools; comfortable in the data layer (SQL, HRIS) and translating between the people side and the technical side.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: People analytics isn't just about pulling headcount reports—from my experience, it's about structuring data so the People team can actually trust it, ask better questions of it, and act on it. That's the part of the work I keep gravitating toward. At NextGen Healthcare I analyzed 2,000+ employee experience responses and turned them into reports the C-suite could actually decide on, and at ODN Oregon I worked with a team of consultants who partnered with an HR client to audit 300+ leave cases and quantify $362K in costs, refuting assumptions with evidence. This role—definitions, governance, self-service that holds up under scrutiny—is that instinct made into a job. It's the data foundation everything else on a People team depends on, and I genuinely like being the person who keeps it honest.
Why do you want to work at Faire?: Two reasons. First, the mission resonates—I've spent time around small independent businesses, and Faire giving them a real shot against big-box competition is the kind of work I'd be glad to support from behind the data. Second, this is squarely how I work: I treat people questions as systems to diagnose, not numbers to report out. I'm also genuinely energized that this role sits at the People-and-IT seam, building toward AI-augmented tools. I lean on AI tools daily and have trained 600+ people on adopting new products, so I'm fluent translating between the people side and the technical side—which is exactly what an AI roadmap built on cross-functional collaboration needs.
What excites you about this opportunity?: Honestly, the chance to build the layer that makes everything else trustworthy. A lot of People teams act on assumptions because the data isn't legible or governed well enough to challenge them—and that's the gap I love closing. At ODN Oregon, helping a client team move from 'we think leave costs are X' to 'we audited 300+ cases and it's $362K'—work I did alongside a team of consultants—changed what decisions were even possible. Doing that at Faire's scale, and then layering AI on a foundation that's actually clean and governed, is the part I'd get up for. I care as much about the definitions holding up as I do about the dashboard looking good.
Tell us about a time you used data to ch: At NextGen Healthcare I led a manager-effectiveness initiative built on 2,000+ employee experience responses. The instinct in the room was that engagement was a compensation problem. Rather than argue, I structured the data to surface what was actually driving the scores, then translated it into reports a non-technical C-suite could act on—and it pointed clearly to manager capability, not pay. That reframing shaped where they invested in leadership development. It taught me that the real value of people data isn't the dashboard; it's making the evidence legible enough that the conversation shifts from assumption to fact. That's the bar I'd hold myself to here.
